Do not re-run tests after running `fix` or `fmt`.\n    30|\n    31|## TUI style conventions\n    32|\n    33|See `codex-rs/tui/styles.md`.\n    34|\n    35|## TUI code conventions\n    36|\n    37|- Use concise styling helpers from ratatui’s Stylize trait.\n    38|  - Basic spans: use \"text\".into()\n    39|  - Styled spans: use \"text\".red(), \"text\".green(), \"text\".magenta(), \"text\".dim(), etc.\n    40|  - Prefer these over constructing styles with `Span::styled` and `Style` directly.\n    41|  - Example: patch summary file lines\n    42|    - Desired: vec![\"  └ \".into(), \"M\".red(), \" \".dim(), \"tui/src/app.rs\".dim()]\n    43|\n    44|### TUI Styling (ratatui)\n    45|\n    46|- Prefer Stylize helpers: use \"text\".dim(), .bold(), .cyan(), .italic(), .underlined() instead of manual Style where possible.\n    47|- Prefer simple conversions: use \"text\".into() for spans and vec![…].into() for lines; when inference is ambiguous (e.g., Paragraph::new/Cell::from), use Line::from(spans) or Span::from(text).\n    48|- Computed styles: if the Style is computed at runtime, using `Span::styled` is OK (`Span::from(text).set_style(style)` is also acceptable).\n    49|- Avoid hardcoded white: do not use `.white()`; prefer the default foreground (no color).\n    50|- Chaining: combine helpers by chaining for readability (e.g., url.cyan().underlined()).\n    51|- Single items: prefer \"text\".into(); use Line::from(text) or Span::from(text) only when the target type isn’t obvious from context, or when using .into() would require extra type annotations.\n    52|- Building lines: use vec![…].into() to construct a Line when the target type is obvious and no extra type annotations are needed; otherwise use Line::from(vec![…]).\n    53|- Avoid churn: don’t refactor between equivalent forms (Span::styled ↔ set_style, Line::from ↔ .into()) without a clear readability or functional gain; follow file‑local conventions and do not introduce type annotations solely to satisfy .into().\n    54|- Compactness: prefer the form that stays on one line after rustfmt; if only one of Line::from(vec![…]) or vec![…].into() avoids wrapping, choose that. If both wrap, pick the one with fewer wrapped lines.\n    55|\n    56|### Text wrapping\n    57|\n    58|- Always use textwrap::wrap to wrap plain strings.\n    59|- If you have a ratatui Line and you want to wrap it, use the helpers in tui/src/wrapping.rs, e.g. word_wrap_lines / word_wrap_line.\n    60|- If you need to indent wrapped lines, use the initial_indent / subsequent_indent options from RtOptions if you can, rather than writing custom logic.\n    61|- If you have a list of lines and you need to prefix them all with some prefix (optionally different on the first vs subsequent lines), use the `prefix_lines` helper from line_utils.\n    62|\n    63|## Tests\n    64|\n    65|### Snapshot tests\n    66|\n    67|This repo uses snapshot tests (via `insta`), especially in `codex-rs/tui`, to validate rendered output.\n    68|\n    69|**Requirement:** any change that affects user-visible UI (including adding new UI) must include\n    70|corresponding `insta` snapshot coverage (add a new snapshot test if one doesn't exist yet, or\n    71|update the existing snapshot). Import this at the top of the test module if it isn't already.\n    92|- Prefer deep equals comparisons whenever possible. Prefer `codex_utils_cargo_bin::find_resource!` so paths resolve correctly under both Cargo and Bazel runfiles.\n   100|\n   101|### Integration tests (core)\n   102|\n   103|- Prefer the utilities in `core_test_support::responses` when writing end-to-end Codex tests.\n   104|\n   105|- All `mount_sse*` helpers return a `ResponseMock`; hold onto it so you can assert against outbound `/responses` POST bodies.\n   106|- Use `ResponseMock::single_request()` when a test should only issue one POST, or `ResponseMock::requests()` to inspect every captured `ResponsesRequest`.\n   107|- `ResponsesRequest` exposes helpers (`body_json`, `input`, `function_call_output`, `custom_tool_call_output`, `call_output`, `header`, `path`, `query_param`) so assertions can target structured payloads instead of manual JSON digging.\n   108|- Build SSE payloads with the provided `ev_*` constructors and the `sse(...)`.\n   109|- Prefer `wait_for_event` over `wait_for_event_with_timeout`.\n   110|- Prefer `mount_sse_once` over `mount_sse_once_match` or `mount_sse_sequence`\n   111|\n   112|- Typical pattern:\n   113|\n   114|  ```rust\n   115|  let mock = responses::mount_sse_once(\u0026server, responses::sse(vec![\n   116|      responses::ev_response_created(\"resp-1\"),\n   117|      responses::ev_function_call(call_id, \"shell\", \u0026serde_json::to_string(\u0026args)?),\n   118|      responses::ev_completed(\"resp-1\"),\n   119|  ])).await;\n   120|\n   121|  codex.submit(Op::UserTurn { ... }).await?;\n   122|\n   123|  // Assert request body if needed.\n   124|  let request = mock.single_request();\n   125|  // assert using request.function_call_output(call_id) or request.json_body() or other helpers.\n   126|  ```\n   127|\n   128|## App-server API Development Best Practices\n   129|\n   130|These guidelines apply to app-server protocol work in `codex-rs`, especially:\n   131|\n   132|- `app-server-protocol/src/protocol/common.rs`\n   133|- `app-server-protocol/src/protocol/v2.rs`\n   134|- `app-server/README.md`\n   135|\n   136|### Core Rules\n   137|\n   138|- All active API development should happen in app-server v2. Do not add new API surface area to v1.\n   139|- Follow payload naming consistently:\n   140|  `*Params` for request payloads, `*Response` for responses, and `*Notification` for notifications.\n   141|- Expose RPC methods as `\u003cresource\u003e/\u003cmethod\u003e` and keep `\u003cresource\u003e` singular (for example, `thread/read`, `app/list`).\n   142|- Always expose fields as camelCase on the wire with `#[serde(rename_all = \"camelCase\")]` unless a tagged union or explicit compatibility requirement needs a targeted rename.\n   143|- Exception: config RPC payloads are expected to use snake_case to mirror config.toml keys (see the config read/write/list APIs in `app-server-protocol/src/protocol/v2.rs`).\n   144|- Always set `#[ts(export_to = \"v2/\")]` on v2 request/response/notification types so generated TypeScript lands in the correct namespace.\n   145|- Never use `#[serde(skip_serializing_if = \"Option::is_none\")]` for v2 API payload fields.\n   146|  Exception: client-\u003eserver requests that intentionally have no params may use:\n   147|  `params: #[ts(type = \"undefined\")] #[serde(skip_serializing_if = \"Option::is_none\")] Option\u003c()\u003e`.\n   148|- Keep Rust and TS wire renames aligned. If a field or variant uses `#[serde(rename = \"...\")]`, add matching `#[ts(rename = \"...\")]`.\n   149|- For discriminated unions, use explicit tagging in both serializers:\n   150|  `#[serde(tag = \"type\", ...)]` and `#[ts(tag = \"type\", ...)]`.\n   151|- Prefer plain `String` IDs at the API boundary (do UUID parsing/conversion internally if needed).\n   152|- Timestamps should be integer Unix seconds (`i64`) and named `*_at` (for example, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `resets_at`).\n   153|- For experimental API surface area:\n   154|  use `#[experimental(\"method/or/field\")]`, derive `ExperimentalApi` when field-level gating is needed, and use `inspect_params: true` in `common.rs` when only some fields of a method are experimental.\n   155|\n   156|### Client-\u003eserver request payloads (`*Params`)\n   157|\n   158|- Every optional field must be annotated with `#[ts(optional = nullable)]`. Do not use `#[ts(optional = nullable)]` outside client-\u003eserver request payloads (`*Params`).\n   159|- Optional collection fields (for example `Vec`, `HashMap`) must use `Option\u003c...\u003e` + `#[ts(optional = nullable)]`. Do not use `#[serde(default)]` to model optional collections, and do not use `skip_serializing_if` on v2 payload fields.\n   160|- When you want omission to mean `false` for boolean fields, use `#[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = \"std::ops::Not::not\")] pub field: bool` over `Option\u003cbool\u003e`.\n   161|- For new list methods, implement cursor pagination by default:\n   162|  request fields `pub cursor: Option\u003cString\u003e` and `pub limit: Option\u003cu32\u003e`,\n   163|  response fields `pub data: Vec\u003c...\u003e` and `pub next_cursor: Option\u003cString\u003e`.\n   164|\n   165|### Development Workflow\n   166|\n   167|- Update docs/examples when API behavior changes (at minimum `app-server/README.md`).\n   168|- Regenerate schema fixtures when API shapes change:\n   169|  `just write-app-server-schema`\n   170|  (and `just write-app-server-schema --experimental` when experimental API fixtures are affected).\n   171|- Validate with `cargo test -p codex-app-server-protocol`.\n   172|- Avoid boilerplate tests that only assert experimental field markers for individual\n   173|  request fields in `common.rs`; rely on schema generation/tests and behavioral coverage instead.\n\u003c/file\u003e\n","data":"","mime_type":"","metadata":"{\"file_path\":\"/Users/af/codex_app_reverse_engineer/codex-oss/AGENTS.md\",\"content\":\"# Rust/codex-rs\\n\\nIn the codex-rs folder where the rust code lives:\\n\\n- Crate names are prefixed with `codex-`. Any existing code that uses `CODEX_SANDBOX_NETWORK_DISABLED_ENV_VAR` was authored with this fact in mind. It is often used to early exit out of tests that the author knew you would not be able to run given your sandbox limitations.\\n  - Similarly, when you spawn a process using Seatbelt (`/usr/bin/sandbox-exec`), `CODEX_SANDBOX=seatbelt` will be set on the child process. Integration tests that want to run Seatbelt themselves cannot be run under Seatbelt, so checks for `CODEX_SANDBOX=seatbelt` are also often used to early exit out of tests, as appropriate.\\n- Always collapse if statements per https://rust-lang.github.io/rust-clippy/master/index.html#collapsible_if\\n- Always inline format! args when possible per https://rust-lang.github.io/rust-clippy/master/index.html#uninlined_format_args\\n- Use method references over closures when possible per https://rust-lang.github.io/rust-clippy/master/index.html#redundant_closure_for_method_calls\\n- When possible, make `match` statements exhaustive and avoid wildcard arms.\\n- When writing tests, prefer comparing the equality of entire objects over fields one by one.\\n- When making a change that adds or changes an API, ensure that the documentation in the `docs/` folder is up to date if applicable.\\n- If you change `ConfigToml` or nested config types, run `just write-config-schema` to update `codex-rs/core/config.schema.json`.\\n- If you change Rust dependencies (`Cargo.toml` or `Cargo.lock`), run `just bazel-lock-update` from the\\n  repo root to refresh `MODULE.bazel.lock`, and include that lockfile update in the same change.\\n- After dependency changes, run `just bazel-lock-check` from the repo root so lockfile drift is caught\\n  locally before CI.\\n- Do not create small helper methods that are referenced only once.\\n\\nRun `just fmt` (in `codex-rs` directory) automatically after you have finished making Rust code changes; do not ask for approval to run it. Additionally, run the tests:\\n\\n1. Run the test for the specific project that was changed. For example, if changes were made in `codex-rs/tui`, run `cargo test -p codex-tui`.\\n2. Once those pass, if any changes were made in common, core, or protocol, run the complete test suite with `cargo test` (or `just test` if `cargo-nextest` is installed). Avoid `--all-features` for routine local runs because it expands the build matrix and can significantly increase `target/` disk usage; use it only when you specifically need full feature coverage. project-specific or individual tests can be run without asking the user, but do ask the user before running the complete test suite.\\n\\nBefore finalizing a large change to `codex-rs`, run `just fix -p \\u003cproject\\u003e` (in `codex-rs` directory) to fix any linter issues in the code. Prefer scoping with `-p` to avoid slow workspace‑wide Clippy builds; only run `just fix` without `-p` if you changed shared crates. Do not re-run tests after running `fix` or `fmt`.\\n\\n## TUI style conventions\\n\\nSee `codex-rs/tui/styles.md`.\\n\\n## TUI code conventions\\n\\n- Use concise styling helpers from ratatui’s Stylize trait.\\n  - Basic spans: use \\\"text\\\".into()\\n  - Styled spans: use \\\"text\\\".red(), \\\"text\\\".green(), \\\"text\\\".magenta(), \\\"text\\\".dim(), etc.\\n  - Prefer these over constructing styles with `Span::styled` and `Style` directly.\\n  - Example: patch summary file lines\\n    - Desired: vec![\\\"  └ \\\".into(), \\\"M\\\".red(), \\\" \\\".dim(), \\\"tui/src/app.rs\\\".dim()]\\n\\n### TUI Styling (ratatui)\\n\\n- Prefer Stylize helpers: use \\\"text\\\".dim(), .bold(), .cyan(), .italic(), .underlined() instead of manual Style where possible.\\n- Prefer simple conversions: use \\\"text\\\".into() for spans and vec![…].into() for lines; when inference is ambiguous (e.g., Paragraph::new/Cell::from), use Line::from(spans) or Span::from(text).\\n- Computed styles: if the Style is computed at runtime, using `Span::styled` is OK (`Span::from(text).set_style(style)` is also acceptable).\\n- Avoid hardcoded white: do not use `.white()`; prefer the default foreground (no color).\\n- Chaining: combine helpers by chaining for readability (e.g., url.cyan().underlined()).\\n- Single items: prefer \\\"text\\\".into(); use Line::from(text) or Span::from(text) only when the target type isn’t obvious from context, or when using .into() would require extra type annotations.\\n- Building lines: use vec![…].into() to construct a Line when the target type is obvious and no extra type annotations are needed; otherwise use Line::from(vec![…]).\\n- Avoid churn: don’t refactor between equivalent forms (Span::styled ↔ set_style, Line::from ↔ .into()) without a clear readability or functional gain; follow file‑local conventions and do not introduce type annotations solely to satisfy .into().\\n- Compactness: prefer the form that stays on one line after rustfmt; if only one of Line::from(vec![…]) or vec![…].into() avoids wrapping, choose that. If both wrap, pick the one with fewer wrapped lines.\\n\\n### Text wrapping\\n\\n- Always use textwrap::wrap to wrap plain strings.\\n- If you have a ratatui Line and you want to wrap it, use the helpers in tui/src/wrapping.rs, e.g. word_wrap_lines / word_wrap_line.\\n- If you need to indent wrapped lines, use the initial_indent / subsequent_indent options from RtOptions if you can, rather than writing custom logic.\\n- If you have a list of lines and you need to prefix them all with some prefix (optionally different on the first vs subsequent lines), use the `prefix_lines` helper from line_utils.\\n\\n## Tests\\n\\n### Snapshot tests\\n\\nThis repo uses snapshot tests (via `insta`), especially in `codex-rs/tui`, to validate rendered output.\\n\\n**Requirement:** any change that affects user-visible UI (including adding new UI) must include\\ncorresponding `insta` snapshot coverage (add a new snapshot test if one doesn't exist yet, or\\nupdate the existing snapshot). Review and accept snapshot updates as part of the PR so UI impact\\nis easy to review and future diffs stay visual.\\n\\nWhen UI or text output changes intentionally, update the snapshots as follows:\\n\\n- Run tests to generate any updated snapshots:\\n  - `cargo test -p codex-tui`\\n- Check what’s pending:\\n  - `cargo insta pending-snapshots -p codex-tui`\\n- Review changes by reading the generated `*.snap.new` files directly in the repo, or preview a specific file:\\n  - `cargo insta show -p codex-tui path/to/file.snap.new`\\n- Only if you intend to accept all new snapshots in this crate, run:\\n  - `cargo insta accept -p codex-tui`\\n\\nIf you don’t have the tool:\\n\\n- `cargo install cargo-insta`\\n\\n### Test assertions\\n\\n- Tests should use pretty_assertions::assert_eq for clearer diffs. Import this at the top of the test module if it isn't already.\\n- Prefer deep equals comparisons whenever possible. Perform `assert_eq!()` on entire objects, rather than individual fields.\\n- Avoid mutating process environment in tests; prefer passing environment-derived flags or dependencies from above.\\n\\n### Spawning workspace binaries in tests (Cargo vs Bazel)\\n\\n- Prefer `codex_utils_cargo_bin::cargo_bin(\\\"...\\\")` over `assert_cmd::Command::cargo_bin(...)` or `escargot` when tests need to spawn first-party binaries.\\n  - Under Bazel, binaries and resources may live under runfiles; use `codex_utils_cargo_bin::cargo_bin` to resolve absolute paths that remain stable after `chdir`.\\n- When locating fixture files or test resources under Bazel, avoid `env!(\\\"CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR\\\")`. Prefer `codex_utils_cargo_bin::find_resource!` so paths resolve correctly under both Cargo and Bazel runfiles.\\n\\n### Integration tests (core)\\n\\n- Prefer the utilities in `core_test_support::responses` when writing end-to-end Codex tests.\\n\\n- All `mount_sse*` helpers return a `ResponseMock`; hold onto it so you can assert against outbound `/responses` POST bodies.\\n- Use `ResponseMock::single_request()` when a test should only issue one POST, or `ResponseMock::requests()` to inspect every captured `ResponsesRequest`.\\n- `ResponsesRequest` exposes helpers (`body_json`, `input`, `function_call_output`, `custom_tool_call_output`, `call_output`, `header`, `path`, `query_param`) so assertions can target structured payloads instead of manual JSON digging.\\n- Build SSE payloads with the provided `ev_*` constructors and the `sse(...)`.\\n- Prefer `wait_for_event` over `wait_for_event_with_timeout`.\\n- Prefer `mount_sse_once` over `mount_sse_once_match` or `mount_sse_sequence`\\n\\n- Typical pattern:\\n\\n  ```rust\\n  let mock = responses::mount_sse_once(\\u0026server, responses::sse(vec![\\n      responses::ev_response_created(\\\"resp-1\\\"),\\n      responses::ev_function_call(call_id, \\\"shell\\\", \\u0026serde_json::to_string(\\u0026args)?),\\n      responses::ev_completed(\\\"resp-1\\\"),\\n  ])).await;\\n\\n  codex.submit(Op::UserTurn { ... }).await?;\\n\\n  // Assert request body if needed.\\n  let request = mock.single_request();\\n  // assert using request.function_call_output(call_id) or request.json_body() or other helpers.\\n  ```\\n\\n## App-server API Development Best Practices\\n\\nThese guidelines apply to app-server protocol work in `codex-rs`, especially:\\n\\n- `app-server-protocol/src/protocol/common.rs`\\n- `app-server-protocol/src/protocol/v2.rs`\\n- `app-server/README.md`\\n\\n### Core Rules\\n\\n- All active API development should happen in app-server v2. Do not add new API surface area to v1.\\n- Follow payload naming consistently:\\n  `*Params` for request payloads, `*Response` for responses, and `*Notification` for notifications.\\n- Expose RPC methods as `\\u003cresource\\u003e/\\u003cmethod\\u003e` and keep `\\u003cresource\\u003e` singular (for example, `thread/read`, `app/list`).\\n- Always expose fields as camelCase on the wire with `#[serde(rename_all = \\\"camelCase\\\")]` unless a tagged union or explicit compatibility requirement needs a targeted rename.\\n- Exception: config RPC payloads are expected to use snake_case to mirror config.toml keys (see the config read/write/list APIs in `app-server-protocol/src/protocol/v2.rs`).\\n- Always set `#[ts(export_to = \\\"v2/\\\")]` on v2 request/response/notification types so generated TypeScript lands in the correct namespace.\\n- Never use `#[serde(skip_serializing_if = \\\"Option::is_none\\\")]` for v2 API payload fields.\\n  Exception: client-\\u003eserver requests that intentionally have no params may use:\\n  `params: #[ts(type = \\\"undefined\\\")] #[serde(skip_serializing_if = \\\"Option::is_none\\\")] Option\\u003c()\\u003e`.\\n- Keep Rust and TS wire renames aligned. If a field or variant uses `#[serde(rename = \\\"...\\\")]`, add matching `#[ts(rename = \\\"...\\\")]`.\\n- For discriminated unions, use explicit tagging in both serializers:\\n  `#[serde(tag = \\\"type\\\", ...)]` and `#[ts(tag = \\\"type\\\", ...)]`.\\n- Prefer plain `String` IDs at the API boundary (do UUID parsing/conversion internally if needed).\\n- Timestamps should be integer Unix seconds (`i64`) and named `*_at` (for example, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `resets_at`).\\n- For experimental API surface area:\\n  use `#[experimental(\\\"method/or/field\\\")]`, derive `ExperimentalApi` when field-level gating is needed, and use `inspect_params: true` in `common.rs` when only some fields of a method are experimental.\\n\\n### Client-\\u003eserver request payloads (`*Params`)\\n\\n- Every optional field must be annotated with `#[ts(optional = nullable)]`. Do not use `#[ts(optional = nullable)]` outside client-\\u003eserver request payloads (`*Params`).\\n- Optional collection fields (for example `Vec`, `HashMap`) must use `Option\\u003c...\\u003e` + `#[ts(optional = nullable)]`. Do not use `#[serde(default)]` to model optional collections, and do not use `skip_serializing_if` on v2 payload fields.\\n- When you want omission to mean `false` for boolean fields, use `#[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = \\\"std::ops::Not::not\\\")] pub field: bool` over `Option\\u003cbool\\u003e`.\\n- For new list methods, implement cursor pagination by default:\\n  request fields `pub cursor: Option\\u003cString\\u003e` and `pub limit: Option\\u003cu32\\u003e`,\\n  response fields `pub data: Vec\\u003c...\\u003e` and `pub next_cursor: Option\\u003cString\\u003e`.\\n\\n### Development Workflow\\n\\n- Update docs/examples when API behavior changes (at minimum `app-server/README.md`).\\n- Regenerate schema fixtures when API shapes change:\\n  `just write-app-server-schema`\\n  (and `just write-app-server-schema --experimental` when experimental API fixtures are affected).\\n- Validate with `cargo test -p codex-app-server-protocol`.\\n- Avoid boilerplate tests that only assert experimental field markers for individual\\n  request fields in `common.rs`; rely on schema generation/tests and behavioral coverage instead.\"}","is_error":false}},{"type":"finish","data":{"reason":"stop","time":0}}]

vendor_imports/skills/  # Custom skills\\n```\\n\\n### CLI Config Location\\n\\n```\\n~/.codex/\\n├── config.toml          # Main config file\\n└── history/\\n```\\n\\n### Environment Override\\nCODEX_API_BASE_URL=\\\"https://custom-api.example.com\\\"\\nCODEX_API_ENDPOINT=\\\"localhost\\\"\\nCODEX_CHATGPT_BASE_URL=\\\"https://custom.backend.com\\\"\\n\\n# App Server\\nCODEX_APP_SERVER_WS_URL=\\\"ws://localhost:8080\\\"\\nCODEX_APP_SERVER_FORCE_CLI=\\\"true\\\"\\n\\n# OSS Provider\\nCODEX_OSS_BASE_URL=\\\"http://localhost:8080/v1\\\"\\nCODEX_OSS_PORT=\\\"8080\\\"\\n\\n# Behavior\\nCODEX_HOME=\\\"/custom/path\\\"\\nCODEX_SHELL=\\\"/bin/zsh\\\"\\nCODEX_MAX_LOG_LEVEL=\\\"debug\\\"\\nCODEX_SPARKLE_ENABLED=\\\"false\\\"\\n```\\n\\n## Custom API Endpoints\\n\\n### Method 1: Environment Variable\\n\\n```bash\\nexport CODEX_API_BASE_URL=\\\"https://your-api.com\\\"\\n```\\n\\n### Method 2: config.toml\\n\\n```toml\\n# ~/.codex/config.toml\\nchatgpt_base_url = \\\"https://your-api.com\\\"\\n\\n# Or add custom provider\\nmodel_provider = \\\"custom\\\"\\n\\n[model_providers.custom]\\nname = \\\"Custom Provider\\\"\\nbase_url = \\\"https://api.example.com/v1\\\"\\nenv_key = \\\"CUSTOM_API_KEY\\\"\\nwire_api = \\\"responses\\\"\\nrequires_openai_auth = false\\n```\\n\\n## Local LLM Support\\n\\n### Using Ollama\\n\\n```bash\\nollama serve\\n/Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/codex --oss --local-provider ollama\\n```\\n\\n### Using LM Studio\\n\\n```bash\\nlms server start\\n/Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/codex --oss --local-provider lmstudio\\n```\\n\\n## Skills System\\n\\nSkills are stored in `~/Library/Application Support/Codex/vendor_imports/skills/`\\n\\n### Structure\\n\\n```\\nskill-name/\\n├── SKILL.md          # Required: Skill definition\\n├── agents/\\n│   └── openai.yaml   # OpenAI agent config\\n├── scripts/          # Optional scripts\\n├── references/       # Optional reference docs\\n└── assets/           # Optional assets\\n```\\n\\n## JSON-RPC Protocol\\n\\n### Key Methods\\n\\n- `initialize` - Initialize connection\\n- `thread/start` - Start new conversation\\n- `thread/resume` - Resume conversation\\n- `turn/start` - Send message\\n- `turn/interrupt` - Stop current turn\\n- `skills/list` - List skills\\n- `config/read`, `config/write` - Config management\\n\\n## Key Findings\\n\\n1. **Binary is OSS code**: The Codex.app Rust binary is compiled from the open-source code at github.com/openai/codex\\n2. **Same config system**: Both OSS CLI and App use `~/.codex/config.toml`\\n3. **No hidden endpoints**: The `chatgpt.com/backend-api` URLs are in the public repo\\n4. **Build your own**: You can compile from source with custom modifications\\n\\n## Development Workflow\\n\\n### Modifying the Electron App\\n\\n```bash\\n# Extract app.asar\\nnpx asar extract /Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/app.asar ./app\\n\\n# After modifications, repack\\nnpx asar pack ./app /Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/app.asar\\n\\n# Re-sign (optional but recommended)\\ncodesign --force --deep --sign - /Applications/Codex.app\\n```\\n\\n### Building Linux Fork from Source\\n\\n```bash\\n# Full Docker build\\nmake build\\n\\n# Or manual:\\ncd codex-oss/codex-rs\\ncargo build --release -p codex-app-server\\ncp target/release/codex-app-server ../codex-linux-fork/bin/codex\\ncd ../codex-linux-fork\\nnpm install\\nnpm run rebuild\\nnpm run build:linux\\n```\\n\\n## Documentation\\n\\n- `CODEX_REVERSE_ENGINEERING.md` - Full reverse engineering guide\\n- `BINARY_VS_OSS_COMPARISON.md` - Comparison between binary and OSS\\n- `codex-oss/AGENTS.md` - Agent guidelines for OSS repo\\n\\n## Important Notes\\n\\n- The `app/` and `app-asar-extracted/` directories contain identical content\\n- The Linux fork requires Docker for Engineering Repository\\n+\\n+This repository contains reverse engineering work on OpenAI's Codex.app desktop application, including extracted binaries, the open-source codebase, and a Linux fork.\\n+\\n+## Repository Structure\\n+\\n+```\\n+codex_app_reverse_engineer/\\n+├── app/                    # Extracted app.asar from Codex.app\\n+│   ├── .vite/build/        # Main process (main.js, preload.js)\\n+│   ├── webview/            # Frontend UI (React/Vite)\\n+│   ├── native/             # Native modules (sparkle.node)\\n+│   └── skills/             # Built-in skills\\n+├── app-asar-extracted/     # Duplicate extraction (same as app/)\\n+├── codex-oss/              # Clone of github.com/openai/codex\\n+│   ├── codex-rs/           # Rust backend source\\n+│   │   ├── core/           # Core library (config, auth, client)\\n+│   │   ├── cli/            # CLI binary\\n+│   │   ├── tui/            # TUI interface\\n+│   │   └── app-server/     # App server (WebSocket/JSON-RPC)\\n+│   ├── sdk/                # TypeScript SDK\\n+│   └── shell-tool-mcp/     # MCP server\\n+├── codex-linux-fork/       # Community Linux fork\\n+│   ├── main.js             # Electron main process\\n+│   ├── preload.js          # Preload script\\n+│   ├── webview/            # UI (copied from app/)\\n+│   ├── resources/          # Icons and binaries\\n+│   └── bin/                # Output for compiled codex binary\\n+├── docker/                 # Docker build system\\n+│   ├── Dockerfile          # Ubuntu 22.04 build environment\\n+│   ├── docker-compose.yml  # Build orchestration\\n+│   └── build.sh            # Build script\\n+└── release/                # Output directory for builds\\n+```\\n+\\n+## Build Commands\\n+\\n+### Linux Fork (via Docker)\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+# Build all packages (.deb, AppImage, tarball)\\n+make build\\n+\\n+# Build specific targets\\n+make deb        # .deb package only\\n+make appimage   # AppImage only\\n+make tarball    # tar.gz only\\n+\\n+# Development\\n+make dev        # Run locally (requires Linux)\\n+make shell      # Open shell in build container\\n+\\n+# Cleanup\\n+make clean      # Remove build artifacts\\n+make prune      # Clean + prune Docker caches\\n+```\\n+\\n+### OSS Codex (Rust)\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+cd codex-oss/codex-rs\\n+\\n+# Build CLI\\n+cargo build --release -p codex-tui\\n+\\n+# Build app server (used by Linux fork)\\n+cargo build --release -p codex-app-server\\n+\\n+# Run tests\\n+cargo test                    # All tests\\n+cargo test -p codex-tui       # Specific package\\n+\\n+# With just (task runner)\\n+just codex \\\"prompt\\\"           # Run codex\\n+just fmt                      # Format code\\n+just fix -p \\u003cproject\\u003e         # Fix linter issues\\n+just test                     # Run tests with nextest\\n+just write-config-schema      # Regenerate config schema\\n+```\\n+\\n+### OSS Codex (TypeScript/Node)\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+cd codex-oss\\n+\\n+# Install dependencies\\n+pnpm install\\n+\\n+# Format\\n+pnpm format:fix               # Format all files\\n+```\\n+\\n+## Architecture\\n+\\n+### Codex.app (macOS)\\n+\\n+```\\n+┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\\n+│ Codex.app                                           │\\n+│ /Applications/Codex.app                             │\\n+├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\\n+│ Electron Shell (app.asar, 36MB)                     │\\n+│   └── .vite/build/main.js + webview/ (React UI)     │\\n+│                                                     │\\n+│ Rust Backend (codex binary, 88MB)                   │\\n+│   └── Compiled from codex-oss/codex-rs              │\\n+└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\\n+```\\n+\\n+**Communication**: JSON-RPC 2.0 over WebSocket or stdio\\n+\\n+### Linux Fork\\n+\\n+Same architecture, but:\\n+- Built from source using Docker\\n+- No Sparkle updater\\n+- Outputs: .deb, AppImage, tarball\\n+\\n+## Key Files\\n+\\n+### For Reverse Engineering\\n+\\n+| File | Purpose |\\n+|------|---------|\\n+| `app/.vite/build/main.js` | Electron main process (API URLs, auth) |\\n+| `app/webview/assets/*.js` | Renderer/UI code |\\n+| `app/package.json` | Build config, version info |\\n+| `binary_strings.txt` | Extracted strings from codex binary |\\n+| `binary_urls.txt` | URLs found in binary |\\n+\\n+### For OSS Modifications\\n+\\n+| File | Purpose |\\n+|------|---------|\\n+| `codex-oss/codex-rs/core/src/model_provider_info.rs` | Provider definitions, base URLs |\\n+| `codex-oss/codex-rs/core/src/config/mod.rs` | Config loading, defaults |\\n+| `codex-oss/codex-rs/core/src/auth.rs` | OAuth flow, token handling |\\n+| `codex-oss/codex-rs/core/src/client.rs` | HTTP client, request building |\\n+| `codex-oss/codex-rs/core/config.schema.json` | Config schema |\\n+\\n+## Configuration\\n+\\n+### App Config Location\\n+\\n+```\\n+~/Library/Application Support/Codex/\\n+├── config.json          # User preferences\\n+├── codex.db             # SQLite database\\n+├── auth.json            # Auth tokens\\n+└── vendor_imports/skills/  # Custom skills\\n+```\\n+\\n+### CLI Config Location\\n+\\n+```\\n+~/.codex/\\n+├── config.toml          # Main config file\\n+└── history/\\n+```\\n+\\n+### Environment Variables\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+# API Override\\n+CODEX_API_BASE_URL=\\\"https://custom-api.example.com\\\"\\n+CODEX_API_ENDPOINT=\\\"localhost\\\"\\n+CODEX_CHATGPT_BASE_URL=\\\"https://custom.backend.com\\\"\\n+\\n+# App Server\\n+CODEX_APP_SERVER_WS_URL=\\\"ws://localhost:8080\\\"\\n+CODEX_APP_SERVER_FORCE_CLI=\\\"true\\\"\\n+\\n+# OSS Provider\\n+CODEX_OSS_BASE_URL=\\\"http://localhost:8080/v1\\\"\\n+CODEX_OSS_PORT=\\\"8080\\\"\\n+\\n+# Behavior\\n+CODEX_HOME=\\\"/custom/path\\\"\\n+CODEX_SHELL=\\\"/bin/zsh\\\"\\n+CODEX_MAX_LOG_LEVEL=\\\"debug\\\"\\n+CODEX_SPARKLE_ENABLED=\\\"false\\\"\\n+```\\n+\\n+## Custom API Endpoints\\n+\\n+### Method 1: Environment Variable\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+export CODEX_API_BASE_URL=\\\"https://your-api.com\\\"\\n+```\\n+\\n+### Method 2: config.toml\\n+\\n+```toml\\n+# ~/.codex/config.toml\\n+chatgpt_base_url = \\\"https://your-api.com\\\"\\n+\\n+# Or add custom provider\\n+model_provider = \\\"custom\\\"\\n+\\n+[model_providers.custom]\\n+name = \\\"Custom Provider\\\"\\n+base_url = \\\"https://api.example.com/v1\\\"\\n+env_key = \\\"CUSTOM_API_KEY\\\"\\n+wire_api = \\\"responses\\\"\\n+requires_openai_auth = false\\n+```\\n+\\n+## Local LLM Support\\n+\\n+### Using Ollama\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+ollama serve\\n+/Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/codex --oss --local-provider ollama\\n+```\\n+\\n+### Using LM Studio\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+lms server start\\n+/Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/codex --oss --local-provider lmstudio\\n+```\\n+\\n+## Skills System\\n+\\n+Skills are stored in `~/Library/Application Support/Codex/vendor_imports/skills/`\\n+\\n+### Structure\\n+\\n+```\\n+skill-name/\\n+├── SKILL.md          # Required: Skill definition\\n+├── agents/\\n+│   └── openai.yaml   # OpenAI agent config\\n+├── scripts/          # Optional scripts\\n+├── references/       # Optional reference docs\\n+└── assets/           # Optional assets\\n+```\\n+\\n+## JSON-RPC Protocol\\n+\\n+### Key Methods\\n+\\n+- `initialize` - Initialize connection\\n+- `thread/start` - Start new conversation\\n+- `thread/resume` - Resume conversation\\n+- `turn/start` - Send message\\n+- `turn/interrupt` - Stop current turn\\n+- `skills/list` - List skills\\n+- `config/read`, `config/write` - Config management\\n+\\n+## Key Findings\\n+\\n+1. **Binary is OSS code**: The Codex.app Rust binary is compiled from the open-source code at github.com/openai/codex\\n+2. **Same config system**: Both OSS CLI and App use `~/.codex/config.toml`\\n+3. **No hidden endpoints**: The `chatgpt.com/backend-api` URLs are in the public repo\\n+4. **Build your own**: You can compile from source with custom modifications\\n+\\n+## Development Workflow\\n+\\n+### Modifying the Electron App\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+# Extract
